title: Jupiter’s weather forecast is cloudy with a chance of nukes
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Remapping the origin of modern apes, cancer-fighting eye drops made from pig semen, and more
Brought to you by Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology

Now accepting entries for the US$ 25,000
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology
Application Deadline June 15, 2026
www.eppendorf.com/prize
View this email in your browser
30 March 2026
Today’s SciencePrudence highlights the only academic scientist on Donald Trump’s top scientific advisory council. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including the epic power of Jupiter’s lightning storms and evidence that paleontologists have been looking in the wrong place for modern ape ancestors.
space  |  News from Science
Jupiter’s weather forecast: cloudy with a chance of nukes
Jupiter is wreathed in storms that incandesce with lightning. But determining just how powerful its lightning can get has been tricky because most bolts are obscured by the gas giant’s thick clouds. Now, a study published this month provides a shocking answer: Individual bolts are 100 to 10,000 times more energetic than Earth’s own.

A single bolt of lightning on Earth releases about 1 billion joules of energy. In contrast, Jupiter’s most extreme bolts carry 10 trillion joules of energy, equivalent to 2400 tons of TNT—one-sixth the power of the atomic bomb dropped on Hiroshima, Japan. Based on the observed rates of flashes, Jupiter’s storms can unleash the force of multiple nuclear weapons every minute.

That Jupiter hosts such tremendous light shows isn’t surprising; the Voyager missions detected flashes of lightning during their 1979 flybys of the planet. But to measure how powerful Jupiter’s lightning could get, researchers turned to NASA’s Juno spacecraft, which has orbited the gas giant since 2016. Using the Hubble Space Telescope and photographs from Juno’s own camera, astronomers managed to track several storms with such precision that an instrument onboard Juno picked up the radio wave bursts emitted by individual lightning flashes. The bigger the radio signal, the more powerful the strike.

The study’s estimates might understate the power of Jupiter’s bolts, as lightning releases energy through sound and heat as well as the radiation Juno detected. “It’s like if the entire lightning flash is the Loch Ness Monster,” said study author Michael Wong. “We can only speculate about how big the monster is.”
Read the full story
paleontology  |  Science
Where did evolution start going ape?
An artist’s illustration of Masripithecus moghraensis, which lived around 17 to 18 million years ago and appears closely related to the last common ancestor of all living apes.  mauricio Antón
Scientists have long assumed that modern apes—the group that includes gibbons, orangutans, chimpanzees, gorillas, and humans—first emerged in East Africa. Newly discovered fossils described recently in Science, however, may rewrite that history.

Researchers working at the Wadi Moghra archaeological site in northern Egypt unearthed teeth and jawbones belonging to a previously unknown ape genus and species, which lived around 17 to 18 million years ago during the Early Miocene epoch. “Discovering a fossil ape in this region is both significant and somewhat surprising,” lead study author Shorouq Al-Ashqar told Live Science. The ancient animal, dubbed Masripithecus moghraensis , appears to be closely related to the last common ancestor of all living apes, suggesting this group actually got its start in northern Africa and the Middle East. Based on its large, textured teeth and robust jaw, the team believes M. moghraensis ate a flexible diet of fruit, nuts, and seeds—helping the species thrive during a period of climatic change.

The findings “confirm that paleontologists might have been looking for crown-hominoid ancestors in the wrong place,” paleoanthropologists David Alba and Júlia Arias-Martorell wrote in a related Science Perspective. “This lesson should be taken with humility but also with renewed optimism, as the required fossils to complete the puzzle of Miocene ape evolution may be somewhere out there, waiting to be discovered.”
read the Science Paper
Read the Perspective
medicine  |  Science advances
More than meets the eye
Retinoblastoma, a rare and aggressive form of eye cancer that primarily affects young children, is difficult to treat. This cancer impacts structures at the back of the eye, requiring invasive therapies that can damage delicate tissues and impair vision. Now, scientists have developed a powerful new treatment—one that comes from a truly unexpected source.

To access the retina and other deep eye structures, researchers must breach a series of complex cellular barriers. The authors of a new Science Advances study turned to tiny vesicles called exosomes, which serve as intercellular messengers and are adept at squeezing through gaps between cell membranes thanks to a protein called epidermal growth factor. The team used exosomes derived from pig semen—chosen for their ability to penetrate the reproductive tract—to create eye drops, which they then tested in mice and rabbits. The drops, which also contain folic acid and nanoenzymes designed to locate and destroy tumor cells, effectively penetrated deep into the eye. In mouse models of retinoblastoma, they successfully suppressed tumor growth while preserving retinal function.

Why pigs? As the study authors explain, these animals serve as a relatively cost-effective and abundant source of semen-derived exosomes, and their barrier-busting protein is structurally similar to the kind produced by humans. Of course, the team also stresses the need for careful pathogen screening and other safety measures before this material can be used in large-scale production or clinical studies.
Read the paper
SPONSORED
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology: Call for Entries 2026
This prize is awarded to young scientists for their outstanding contributions to neurobiological research based on experimental methods of molecular, cellular, systems, or organismic biology. Researchers not older than 35 years are invited to apply.
Apply Now
Scienceprudence
Nobel Prize in Physics 2025 laureate U.S. physicist John M. Martinis (left) poses with his award during the Nobel Prize award ceremony on 10 December 2025 in Stockholm, Sweden.  jonathan nackstrand/afp via getty images
Nobelist volunteers to be academics’ pipeline to Trump
Jeffrey Mervis, Senior Correspondent, News from Science
John Martinis admits that being chosen to serve on the country’s most prestigious scientific advisory panel can’t compete with winning a Nobel Prize in physics. But he says the two honors are similar in one respect: Both caught him by surprise.

“I was asked a month ago if I would consider an appointment to PCAST [the President’s Council of Advisors on Science and Technology] and said yes,” says Martinis, a professor at the University of California (UC), Santa Barbara, and co-founder of a quantum computing company called QoLab. “So it was a shock when a colleague came up to me at a conference and congratulated me, because I hadn’t read the email from the White House containing the list of members being announced the next day. It was a bit reminiscent of the call [last October] from the Nobel committee.”

Martinis shared the 2025 physics prize with a mentor and colleague for their work on quantum tunneling, which underpins the burgeoning field of quantum computing, one of President Donald Trump’s highest priorities. Now Trump is asking Martinis and 12 corporate titans, including some of the world’s wealthiest men, to advise him “on the opportunities and challenges” that quantum and other “emerging technologies present to the American workforce.”

Martinis says he plans to “listen to and learn from” the likes of Meta’s Mark Zuckerberg, Oracle’s Larry Ellison, Nvidia’s Jensen Huang, Google’s Sergey Brin, and ADM’s Lisa Su. “They’re obviously very smart and successful business leaders,” Martinis says. “And since I have both an academic and entrepreneurial background [he spent 5 years at Google before returning to academia in 2020], I think I will fit in.”

But Martinis says he will also champion the role of fundamental research such as his in enabling those new technologies. “I received a congratulatory note from the UC system, and one of the things they liked was that I’ll get to represent academia in PCAST,” says Martinis. “That’s certainly part of what I want to do. And I invite scientists to contact me so that I can share their views.”

Many academic scientists are hoping Martinis won’t be the sole voice for their sector. “I hope that future appointments will expand PCAST expertise to include broader science and engineering disciplines and viewpoints that can contribute to the nation,” says chemist Frances Arnold of the California Institute of Technology, a fellow Nobelist and former PCAST co-chair under President Joe Biden.

Martinis has been to the White House twice, once in 2019 to tout Google’s claim for “quantum supremacy” and again last year after winning the Nobel Prize. Both times he met with Michael Kratsios, Trump’s science advisor and co-chair of PCAST, and came away impressed. “I felt that he was someone I could work with.”

Martinis declined to say whether he thought the deep cuts in research spending that Trump proposed last year—and Congress largely rejected—would have damaged the U.S. scientific enterprise if enacted. “I’m not an expert on the administration’s policy toward science, so I don’t think I should comment on that,” he says. “My role is to listen to other people’s opinions and then make my recommendations.”
Read more about the panel
Et Cetera
Skin deep
Why do inflammatory skin conditions like psoriasis tend to flare up in the same spots? According to a new mouse study, skin cells hold on to patterns of gene expression every time they regenerate—retaining a “memory” of past inflammation.
Science Paper  |  Read more at Scientific American
Lost in space
Sperm cells exposed to microgravity in experiments struggled to find their way through a maze designed to simulate the female reproductive tract, suggesting potential hurdles for reproduction in space. “They don’t really know which way is up or down,” one researcher said.
Communications Biology Paper  |  Read more at The Guardian
Mouse milestone
Scientists have developed a new technique that measures gene expression across whole-body cross-sections from mice. The method could reveal bodywide effects of drugs and diseases, potentially paving the way for better treatments.
Cell Paper  |  Read more at News from Science
The SRY gene alone should not determine who can compete in women’s sport.
—Andrew Sinclair, University of Melbourne
EXPERT REACTION  |  27 March 2026  |  scimex
Sinclair—who identified the sex determining region Y (SRY) gene—reacting to the International Olympic Committee announcement that participation in female Olympic events will be limited to biological females as determined by the absence of the SRY gene, typically found in males. Sinclair explains that although SRY has a strong influence over sex, there are sundry factors that steer development. “This policy is based on the overly simplistic idea that the presence of the SRY gene alone is equivalent to being male,“ he said. Yet, “ it does not tell you how SRY is functioning, whether a testis has formed, whether testosterone is produced and, if so, whether it can be used by the body,” he explained in an article for The Conversation last year.
Last but not least
Although “cow flu” may have faded from the headlines, the H5N1 influenza virus continues to circulate in cattle. The worst part? Scientists still aren’t sure how it spreads.
Phie Jacobs, General Assignment Reporter, Science

With contributions from Michael Greshko and Jeffrey Mervis
